title: File:DIG13762-001 - "Ladies and Gentlemen... the Beatles!" exhibit at LBJ Presidential Library, Austin, TX, 2015-06-12 10.55.39.jpg
creator: Lauren Gerson Uploaded on Flickr by LBJ
description: "Ladies and Gentlemen...The Beatles!", a traveling exhibit curated by the GRAMMY Museum® and Fab Four Exhibits, is open at the LBJ Presidential Library from June 13, 2015 through January 10, 2016. More than 400 pieces of memorabilia, records, rare photographs, tour artifacts, video, and instruments are on display, including John Lennon's missing Gibson J-160E guitar. The guitar is on display for two weeks only, from June 13 until June 29, 2015. Photo by Lauren Gerson. Beatles Logo Bass Drum Head used for The Ed Sullivan Show , February 1964 In January 1964, Beatles manager Brian Epstein contacted London drum retailer Drum City to have a new Beatles logo drumhead hand painted by local graphic artist Edwin “ Eddie ” Stakers , who had painted Ringo's First logo drumhead for their upcoming first visit to America. This logo drumhead was used for all three Ed Sullivan Show appearances, the Washington Coliseum show and the two Carnegie Hall concerts during the band's first American visit. After returning to England, this drumhead was retired to EMI's Abbey Road Studios for recording purposes and not seen publicly again. Courtesy of Russ Lease/fabfourexhibits.com MEET THE BRITAIN'S “BEATLEMANIA” HAS SPREAD TO AMERICA! ON TV: Jack Paar Show (Jan. 3, NBC TV)! Ed Sullivan Show (Feb. 9, Feb. 16, CBS-TV)! Already seen on Walter Cronkite News (CBS-TV), Huntley-Brinkley News (NBC-TV)! Feature in Time, Life, Newsweek and newspapers everywhere! Among record buyers “Beatlemania” has proved absolutely contagious. Over 3,000,000 discs already sold in England alone. So be prepared for the kind of sales epidemic that made THE BEATLES the biggest-selling vocal group in British history! CALL YOUR CAPITOL SALES REP, TODAY! BEATLES! FIRST CAPTOL SINGLE I WANT TO HOLD YOUR HAND b/w I SAW HER STANDING THERE FIRST CAPITOL ALBUM MEET THE BEATLES Ladies and Gentlemen... TH E BE A T L E S ! Washington Coliseum The First American Concert The Beatles' first-ever American concert occurred in Washington, D.C. on February 11, 1964, four days after their arrival in America. Tickets for the sold-out concert were $2, $3 and $5. Performing for just over eight thousand, mostly screaming fans at the Washington Coliseum, the group's thirty-one minute, twelve-song set included “I Want to Hold Your Hand,” “She Loves You,” “All My Loving,” “Twist and Shout,” and “Please Please Me.” Also on the bill that night were Tommy Roe, the Chiffons, Jay and the Americans, the Righteous Brothers, and the Varavelles. When the show was over, John Lennon remarked, “Tonight was marvelous! Ridiculous! Eight thousand people all shouting at once. We were trying to shout louder than them with microphones and we still couldn't beat them!” The following day, The Beatles returned to New York City via train to perform two evening shows at Carnegie Hall. CBS captured the Washington, D.C. concert on film and presented it on March 14 and 15 as a closed circuit telecast in theaters around the country. Ladies and Gentlemen... TH E BE A T L E S ! Carnegie Hall After playing Washington, D.C., The Beatles returned to New York City on February 12 for two sold-out shows at Carnegie Hall. The Brianwoods, an American folk group with no connection to rock & roll, opened both the early and late show. The Beatles performed for little over a half hour at each, playing much of their Washington, D.C. set to the delight of some six thousand fans who were fortunate enough to have purchased tickets, priced $3 to $5.50. Despite playing one of the most esteemed concert venues in America, hearing The Beatles proved challenging at Carnegie Hall like it it would everywhere else the group performed in America over the next couple of years. Screaming fans and a primitive rock concert sound system prevented The Beatles' songs from arching over the screaming hysteria in the audience.
